description: UE5 collision system: line traces, sweep traces, FHitResult, collision channels, collision responses, overlap events, and physics simulation

entries.title: Line Traces (Raycasts)
entries.description: Line traces cast an invisible ray from point A to point B and report what it hits. The most common way to detect objects in the world — used for shooting, interaction, ground detection, and more.
entries.whatItIs: A line trace (raycast) is a collision query that fires an infinitely thin ray between two world-space points and returns information about the first (or all) blocking surfaces it intersects. It is the cheapest and most commonly used spatial query in UE5.
whenToUse: Hitscan weapon firing — checking what a bullet would hit instantly
Interaction systems — detecting what the player is looking at
Ground detection — checking the surface below a character
Line-of-sight checks — determining if one actor can see another
Placement previews — projecting objects onto surfaces for building systems
Camera obstruction — detecting walls between camera and character
whenNotToUse: When you need a wider detection area — use sweep traces instead (e.g., melee attacks, aim assist)
When you need to detect everything in a volume — use overlap queries instead
Per-tick traces on hundreds of actors — consider spatial partitioning or collision events instead
When you need continuous collision detection for fast-moving objects — use physics simulation with CCD enabled
commonMistakes: Forgetting to call AddIgnoredActor(this) — the trace hits the actor that fired it
Using ECC_Visibility when you should use ECC_Camera or a custom channel, leading to incorrect hit filtering
Not checking if GetActor() returns nullptr — BSP geometry and landscape can be hit without an owning actor
Setting bTraceComplex=true everywhere — per-poly traces are significantly more expensive and rarely needed
Tracing from the character mesh instead of the camera — causes hits behind walls the player cannot see
Using LineTraceMulti when you only need the first hit — wastes performance collecting unnecessary results
keyPoints: LineTraceSingleByChannel(): traces against a collision channel, returns first hit
LineTraceSingleByObjectType(): traces against specific object types (WorldStatic, Pawn, etc.)
LineTraceMultiByChannel(): returns ALL hits along the line, not just the first
Parameters: Start, End, CollisionChannel, FCollisionQueryParams
FCollisionQueryParams: AddIgnoredActor(), bTraceComplex (per-poly vs simple collision)
Common channels: ECC_Visibility (visible objects), ECC_Camera (camera collision), ECC_Pawn (characters)
Returns bool (hit or not) and fills FHitResult with details
DrawDebugLine() to visualize traces during development

entries.title: Sweep Traces (Shape Traces)
entries.description: Sweep traces move a shape (sphere, box, capsule) from start to end and report what it hits. More forgiving than line traces — great for melee attacks, character detection, and thick raycasts.
entries.whatItIs: A sweep trace moves a collision shape (sphere, box, or capsule) along a path from start to end, reporting any surfaces the shape intersects. It functions like a thick raycast that accounts for the spatial volume of the swept shape.
whenToUse: Melee attack detection — sweeping a sphere or box in front of the character
Aim assist — using a wide sphere trace to be more forgiving than a thin line trace
Character fit-testing — sweeping a capsule to check if the player can fit through a space or stand up from crouch
Projectile simulation — tracing a sphere along the projectile path for accurate hit detection
Ground detection with tolerance — sphere traces handle uneven terrain better than line traces
Item pickup detection — sweeping a sphere to find nearby interactable objects
whenNotToUse: When a simple line trace would suffice — sweeps are more expensive than line traces
When you need to detect all actors inside a volume without movement — use OverlapMulti instead
When the shape radius is zero — just use a line trace, it is cheaper
For very long-range hitscan weapons where precision matters more than forgiveness
commonMistakes: Using an overly large sweep radius — detects things the player clearly did not aim at, feels unfair
Forgetting to pass FQuat::Identity for rotation — uninitialized rotation causes unpredictable results
Not realizing that Start == End is invalid for sweeps — use OverlapMulti for zero-length volume queries
Using MakeBox with full extents instead of half extents — the box ends up twice the intended size
Not ignoring the owning actor — the sweep immediately hits the actor performing it
Sweeping with bTraceComplex on skeletal meshes — extremely expensive and usually unnecessary
keyPoints: SweepSingleByChannel(): sweep a shape, return first hit
SweepMultiByChannel(): sweep a shape, return all hits
Shape types: FCollisionShape::MakeSphere(Radius), MakeBox(HalfExtent), MakeCapsule(Radius, HalfHeight)
SphereTraceSingle (KismetSystemLibrary): sweep trace with built-in debug drawing
Use sphere traces for: melee attacks, aim-assist, picking up items, ground checks
Use capsule traces for: character fit-testing (can the player fit here?)
SweepSingleByProfile(): trace against a named collision profile
More expensive than line traces but more accurate for gameplay interactions

entries.title: FHitResult — Trace Result Data
entries.description: FHitResult contains all the information about what a trace or sweep hit: the actor, component, location, normal, bone name, and more.
entries.whatItIs: FHitResult is a struct that stores the complete output of a collision query (line trace, sweep, or overlap). It contains the hit location, surface normal, the actor and component that were hit, the bone name for skeletal meshes, and the physical material at the impact point.
whenToUse: Whenever you perform any line trace or sweep trace — FHitResult is the output struct
Applying point damage — ApplyPointDamage requires the FHitResult for hit bone and location
Spawning impact effects — use ImpactPoint and ImpactNormal to position decals and particles
Headshot or limb-specific damage — read BoneName to determine which body part was hit
Surface-specific audio and VFX — read PhysMaterial to determine surface type
whenNotToUse: When you only need a boolean hit/miss check — you still get FHitResult but can ignore its fields
For overlap queries that return FOverlapResult instead — different struct with different fields
Do not cache FHitResult long-term — the referenced actor or component may be destroyed
commonMistakes: Confusing ImpactPoint and Location — ImpactPoint is where the surface was hit, Location is where the trace shape center was at impact time
Confusing ImpactNormal and Normal — ImpactNormal is the surface normal, Normal is the trace shape normal
Not checking if GetActor() is nullptr before using the returned pointer — world geometry may not have an actor
Assuming BoneName is always valid — it is NAME_None for static meshes and non-skeletal geometry
Not checking PhysMaterial.IsValid() before dereferencing — not all surfaces have a physical material assigned
Reading FHitResult fields when the trace returned false (bBlockingHit is false) — the data is uninitialized
keyPoints: bBlockingHit: true if the trace hit something that blocks
GetActor(): the actor that was hit (can be nullptr for world geo without an actor)
GetComponent(): the specific UPrimitiveComponent that was hit
ImpactPoint: the exact world location where the trace hit the surface
ImpactNormal: the surface normal at the impact point (useful for decals, reflections)
Location: the location of the trace shape at the moment of impact (differs from ImpactPoint for sweeps)
Normal: the normal of the trace shape at impact
Distance: how far along the trace the hit occurred
BoneName: for skeletal meshes, which bone was hit (headshot detection!)
PhysMaterial: the physical material at the hit point (for surface-specific effects)

entries.title: Collision Channels & Responses
entries.description: Collision channels categorize objects, and collision responses determine how objects interact with each other (block, overlap, or ignore).
entries.whatItIs: Collision channels are categories assigned to collision components (e.g., WorldStatic, Pawn, Projectile) and collision responses (Block, Overlap, Ignore) define how each channel interacts with every other channel. Together they form a matrix that governs all collision behavior in UE5.
whenToUse: Setting up any actor that needs collision — every collidable actor needs a channel and response configuration
Creating custom projectile or interaction channels to filter traces precisely
Building trigger volumes that only respond to specific actor types
Separating player collision from AI collision for gameplay purposes
Configuring which objects a camera trace should detect for obstruction
whenNotToUse: Do not create a new custom channel for every actor type — UE5 limits you to 18 custom channels total
Avoid per-channel configuration when a collision preset (profile) already does what you need
Do not use collision channels as a general-purpose tagging system — use Gameplay Tags instead
commonMistakes: Forgetting that both objects must agree on Block for a block to occur — if A blocks B but B ignores A, there is no blocking
Creating too many custom channels and running out of the 18-channel limit
Setting collision responses in the constructor but overriding them with a collision preset in Blueprint, or vice versa
Using SetCollisionResponseToAllChannels(ECR_Block) and then wondering why overlap events do not fire
Not understanding the difference between Object channels and Trace channels — traces use trace channels, physics uses object channels
Setting collision to QueryOnly but expecting physics simulation to work — use QueryAndPhysics if you need both
keyPoints: Built-in channels: WorldStatic, WorldDynamic, Pawn, PhysicsBody, Vehicle, Destructible
Trace channels: Visibility, Camera — used for line/sweep traces
Custom channels: create in Project Settings > Collision > New Object/Trace Channel (max 18 custom)
Collision responses per channel: Block (stops movement), Overlap (generates events), Ignore (no interaction)
Both objects must agree: A blocks B AND B blocks A for a block to occur
Collision Presets: named profiles (NoCollision, BlockAll, OverlapAll, BlockAllDynamic, Pawn, etc.)
SetCollisionProfileName(): apply a preset. SetCollisionResponseToChannel(): set individual responses.
SetCollisionEnabled(): None, QueryOnly (traces only), PhysicsOnly (simulation only), QueryAndPhysics (both)

entries.title: Overlap & Hit Events
entries.description: Collision components generate events when actors overlap or physically collide. These are the primary way to detect gameplay interactions like pickups, triggers, and damage zones.
entries.whatItIs: Overlap and hit events are delegate-based notifications fired by UPrimitiveComponent when collision interactions occur. Overlap events (BeginOverlap/EndOverlap) fire when two components interpenetrate, while hit events fire on blocking physical collisions. They are the event-driven alternative to polling with traces.
whenToUse: Pickup collection — detect when a player walks into an item
Trigger volumes — detect when any actor enters or exits a region
Damage zones — apply damage-over-time while an actor is inside a volume
Projectile impacts — detect when a projectile hits a wall or character using OnComponentHit
Proximity detection — know when enemies are within a certain radius
Physics interactions — react to rigid bodies colliding with surfaces or each other
whenNotToUse: When you need precise per-frame hit information — use traces in Tick instead
For high-frequency physics contacts (e.g., a ball rolling on the ground) — OnComponentHit fires every frame and can be expensive
When you only need a one-time check — use GetOverlappingActors() or a single trace instead of binding persistent events
When neither actor moves — overlap events only fire on movement or spawn, not on static placement
commonMistakes: Forgetting to set GenerateOverlapEvents=true on BOTH components — overlaps require both to opt in
Setting collision response to Block instead of Overlap — Block generates OnComponentHit, not OnComponentBeginOverlap
Binding delegates in the constructor instead of BeginPlay — components may not be fully initialized yet
Not checking the OtherActor class with Cast before using it — any actor type could trigger the overlap
Destroying the actor inside BeginOverlap without deferring — can cause issues if multiple overlaps fire in the same frame
Forgetting that OnComponentHit only fires when physics simulation is active or movement component sweeps — static overlapping objects will not fire hit events
keyPoints: OnComponentBeginOverlap: fired when two components start overlapping. Requires GenerateOverlapEvents=true on BOTH.
OnComponentEndOverlap: fired when two components stop overlapping.
OnComponentHit: fired when a physics collision occurs (blocking hit).
Both actors must have GenerateOverlapEvents=true and compatible collision responses (Overlap, not Ignore).
Bind with AddDynamic: Component->OnComponentBeginOverlap.AddDynamic(this, &MyClass::OnOverlap)
Signature: OnOverlap(UPrimitiveComponent*, AActor*, UPrimitiveComponent*, int32, bool, const FHitResult&)
For triggers: use a UBoxComponent/USphereComponent with Overlap response
GetOverlappingActors(): check current overlaps without waiting for events

entries.title: Physics Simulation
entries.description: UE5 uses the Chaos physics engine for rigid body simulation, constraints, and physical interactions.
entries.whatItIs: UE5's physics simulation is powered by the Chaos physics engine, which handles rigid body dynamics, gravity, forces, impulses, constraints (joints/hinges/springs), and collision resolution. Any UPrimitiveComponent can be made into a simulated physics body at runtime.
whenToUse: Ragdoll effects — switching a character to physics simulation on death
Throwable or launchable objects — grenades, balls, debris that need realistic trajectories
Destructible environments — Chaos destruction for fracturing meshes on impact
Physics puzzles — stacking, balancing, or weight-based gameplay mechanics
Explosive force — applying radial impulses to scatter nearby objects
Vehicles — vehicle simulation relies on underlying physics constraints and forces
whenNotToUse: For precise deterministic movement — physics simulation is non-deterministic and frame-rate sensitive
For player character movement — use UCharacterMovementComponent instead, which handles replication and prediction
When you need exact network replication — physics replication is expensive and imprecise; use movement components for gameplay-critical objects
For simple projectiles that do not need to bounce or ricochet — a ProjectileMovementComponent or manual sweep is cheaper
On hundreds of simultaneous objects — physics simulation is CPU-intensive; pool and limit active bodies
commonMistakes: Enabling physics without collision — SetSimulatePhysics(true) requires collision to be enabled or the object falls through the world
Confusing AddForce and AddImpulse — Force is applied continuously per-frame (call in Tick), Impulse is a one-time push
Not accounting for mass — AddForce scales with mass, so heavy objects barely move; use bAccelChange=true to ignore mass
Setting velocity directly every frame instead of using forces — overrides physics simulation and prevents natural deceleration
Forgetting to set the component as Movable — physics simulation does not work on Static mobility components
Enabling physics on a component that is not the root — child components with physics can behave unexpectedly; make the physics body the root
keyPoints: SetSimulatePhysics(true): enable physics simulation on a UPrimitiveComponent
Physics requires: collision enabled, simple or complex collision, mass set
AddForce(Force), AddImpulse(Impulse): apply forces to physics bodies. Impulse is instant, Force is continuous.
AddForce with bAccelChange=true: ignores mass (applies as acceleration)
SetPhysicsLinearVelocity(), SetPhysicsAngularVelocityInDegrees(): directly set velocity
Mass: auto-calculated from volume and physical material density, or override with SetMassOverrideInKg()
Constraints: UPhysicsConstraintComponent for joints, hinges, springs between bodies
Physical Materials: define friction, restitution (bounciness), density per surface
SetEnableGravity(false): disable gravity on a specific component
Chaos destruction: fracture meshes for destructible environments
